FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Year 2 assessment (2018-2020), N=9,008, ages 10-14

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: cross-sectional linear regression examining associations between problematic social media use (SMAQ) and alcohol expectancies (AEQ-AB positive and negative subscales), adjusted for multiple confounders

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed research article in BMC Public Health, 2023

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: SMAQ (Social Media Addiction Questionnaire) is a qualifying phone/SM-specific variable (problematic social media use scale). Paired with individual-trait-level outcome: alcohol expectancies (beliefs about positive and negative effects of alcohol). Table 2 reports β=0.045 (95% CI 0.020-0.069, p<0.001) for positive expectancies and β=0.072 (95% CI 0.043-0.101, p<0.001) for negative expectancies

FT_IC3.evidence: Quantitative result reported: regression coefficients (β) with 95% confidence intervals and p-values for SM-alcohol expectancies associations

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric results for SM↔outcome relationship reported in Table 2: β coefficients with CIs and significance tests

FT_EC7_reason: Design is cross-sectional (baseline and Year 2 cross-sectional analysis); FT-EC7 carve-out applies: any cross-sectional SM-as-DV analysis disqualifies FT-EC7 regardless of longitudinal presence
notes: Paper meets all inclusion criteria. SM variable (SMAQ) qualifies as phone/SM-specific. Outcome (alcohol expectancies) is individual-trait-level (cognitive/belief construct). Cross-sectional design means FT-EC7 does not apply.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Baseline and Year 2 assessments (2018-2020), N=9,673 screen-using adolescents, ages 11-12

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: prospective generalized logistic and linear mixed effects models examining associations between baseline ACE score and problematic screen use (VGAQ, SMAQ, MPIQ)

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed research article in BMC Public Health, 2023

FT_IC2.evidence: Binding clarification violation: VGAQ (Video Game Addiction Questionnaire), SMAQ (Social Media Addiction Questionnaire), and MPIQ (Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire) are problematic-use scales and thus are SM/phone variables themselves, NOT individual-trait-level outcomes. Per criteria: 'Problematic-use scales are SM/phone variables, not individual-trait outcomes.' The paper analyzes ACEs (individual-trait predictor) → problematic screen use scales (DV). Although ACEs qualify as individual-trait on one side, the other side must be an individual-trait-level NON-SM outcome (neural, behavioral, health, well-being, etc.). Problematic-use scales measure SM/phone use behavior and cannot serve as the qualifying 'other-side' variable. This is analogous to the prohibited pattern: demographic→SMAQ-as-DV, except here it is ACEs→SMAQ/VGAQ/MPIQ-as-DV, which fails the same logic.

notes: Paper fails FT-IC2 because the dependent variable is a problematic-use scale (VGAQ/SMAQ/MPIQ), which measures SM/phone use behavior itself, not an individual-trait-level outcome. Even though ACEs are individual-trait, the pairing does not meet the requirement: SM/phone variable must pair with individual-trait-level outcome on the OTHER side.

FT_IC1.evidence: References U.S. ABCD Study and describes papers analyzing ABCD data from baseline, Years 1, and 2 assessments

FT_IC5.evidence: This is an editorial introduction to a special issue in Health Psychology. It is not an original empirical analysis of ABCD data. The paper introduces and summarizes the scope of other papers in the special issue (e.g., effects of screen time and caffeine on sleep, prenatal tobacco exposure, ACEs and problematic screen use) but does not report new ABCD data analysis or results. Per criteria: 'NOT_MET: Reviews, meta-analyses without new ABCD analysis, editorials, commentaries, perspectives, letters, study protocols, resource/data-availability descriptions, special-issue introductions, book chapters, news pieces.'

notes: Paper fails FT-IC5 (non-empirical): it is an editorial/introduction to a special issue, not an original empirical analysis of ABCD data.
